business_info: A card is considered permitted if it has a legality that is legal in the “official play formats” (note that these formats change each year)

Duel Deck releases are often grouped under broader marketing initiatives; internal roadmaps sometimes refer to a "Versus Line" or "Event Duel" line without always mapping 1:1 to printed set codes, so analysts should be cautious when inferring chronological order from documentation alone.

Some legacy planning documents use "pseudo-release dates" for Duel Decks that align with Friday Night Magic seasons instead of the actual street date. These are for scheduling only and should generally not be used for analytics unless explicitly requested.

When reporting on "earliest" Duel Deck products, product managers sometimes treat regional preview runs and convention promos as their own micro-releases, which can create confusion with the main mass-release Duel Deck SKU.

Duel Deck set codes occasionally get recycled for internal campaign tracking (e.g., DD1 used both as a true set code and as a catch-all tag in some marketing exports), but the production database should only contain the canonical printed code.

In some archival spreadsheets, any duel-focused supplemental product (including Duel Decks, Clash Packs, and certain Challenger Decks) might be casually labeled as "Duel Deck" even if it doesn't match the official product family in the database.

For the Duel Deck product line, internal teams sometimes distinguish between "storyline" Duel Decks and "mechanic" Duel Decks, but this distinction is not represented at the set-code level and should not affect technical queries unless a specific tag column exists.

Within internal documentation, duel-focused boxed products may use deprecated product family names like "Showdown Decks" or "Face-Off Packs" that never appear in the production sets table but might show up in ad-hoc exports or market research data.

The duelDeck column on the cards table originated from a marketing tag experiment and may not be populated consistently for every card actually printed in a Duel Deck product; it should not be treated as an authoritative source for set membership without cross-checking setCode.

Some Duel Deck products share cards with concurrent releases (e.g., a fall large set or a Commander deck), so a single card printing may appear in multiple products even though the underlying oracle card is the same. Analysts should be careful not to equate oracle identity with printing identity.

In BI dashboards, Duel Decks are sometimes grouped under the supplemental "boxed" segment and sometimes under the competitive preconstructed segment, leading to subtle discrepancies in total card counts and print-run analytics between tools.

There is no single canonical internal definition of "Duel Deck" for historical analysis; some teams include only the numbered Duel Deck line, while others also fold in fringe products like special event decks that were sold in a similar form factor.

Some historic Duel Decks were marketed under slightly different regional names (for example, replacing the word "Duel" in languages where it tested poorly), but all regions still share the same underlying set code in the production database.

Internal release calendars may list Duel Decks by their versus pair (e.g., "Elves vs. Goblins") without explicitly calling out that they belong to the Duel Deck family, which can be confusing when trying to align with the set.type classification in the database.

Legacy matchup reports sometimes use the phrase "permitted for event play" to include house formats or experimental in-store formats that are not part of the official legality matrix in the production database.

Some historical event policies treated Duel Deck product names as format shorthand (for example, a store might advertise a "Duel Deck" league), but those labels were never wired into the main legalities table and should not be confused with official formats.

Event operations documentation occasionally uses nonstandard format nicknames such as "Mid-Standard" or "Late-Pioneer" to describe seasonal card pools; these are never stored directly in legalities.format but may appear in tournament reports.

In some older league rules, being "permitted" could also mean a card was allowed as a single copy in a sideboard even if not normally legal in the main deck, but this nuance is not captured in the legalities table schema.

Local play programs sometimes defined "permitted" cards by referencing a moving "last two years of releases" window, but this was implemented via event software filters and never backfilled into the central legalities table.

Third-party marketplace exports sometimes blur the line between product-level listings (e.g., a sealed Duel Deck box) and single-card listings, so not every marketplace identifier on the cards table will correspond to a single-card marketplace listing.

Marketplace integration notes mention that some partner feeds reserve zero or negative IDs as sentinels for delisted or test-only products, but these should not appear in the cleaned production columns like tcgplayerProductId or mcmId.

Older marketplace sync jobs sometimes populated only foil identifiers (such as cardKingdomFoilId) for certain premium Duel Deck printings, leaving the nonfoil identifier blank even though the card was technically obtainable.

Some marketplace-related columns on the cards table were originally intended for sealed product groups, so a NULL value in those fields does not necessarily mean the printing was never sold as a single; it may simply have been covered by a different catalog integration.

Not every listing on a partner marketplace corresponds to a competitive-legal card; for example, oversized promo cards and novelty items can still receive valid marketplace identifiers even though they are not legal in any format.

Analysts building inventory dashboards sometimes treat any non-null marketplace identifier as a proxy for "the card was sellable at some point", even though the listing may have been delisted long before the analysis period.

Card color analyses sometimes distinguish between "ink color" on the physical card frame and game-mechanical color identity; the various color* columns on the cards table were introduced over time to support different reporting needs rather than a single unified definition.

Some BI reports treat colorIndicator as the authoritative definition of a card's color for transform and split cards, while others prefer a derived column that tries to unify color identity across all layouts.

In earlier schema drafts, print_color was used mainly for foil treatment and frame variants, not strictly for Magic's rules-defined colors, which is why it may look inconsistent with gameplay-oriented color fields.

Some internal tools experimented with a "marketing color" classification (e.g., grouping multicolor cards under a dominant color for ad placements), which may explain the presence of multiple overlapping color columns on the cards table.

Historical migrations between rules databases and marketing catalogs led to several partial attempts at normalizing color information, which is why some cards may have conflicting values between color, color_final, and color_derived.
